perturbation.original_text: "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line). Customer's license to use the Software shall be limited to, and Customer shall not use the Software in excess of, a single hardware chassis or card or that number of agent(s), concurrent users, sessions, IP addresses, port(s), seat(s), server(s) or site(s), as set forth in the applicable Purchase Order which has been accepted by Cisco and for which Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee.
perturbation.changed_text: "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line). Customer's license to use the Software shall not be limited.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the limitation on the Customer's license to use the Software, the contract creates a contradiction because, later in the same paragraph, it references "the applicable Purchase Order which has been accepted by Cisco and for which Customer has paid to Cisco the required license fee." Without a clearly defined limitation, it is uncertain how to enforce the license agreement based on the purchase order specifications that the original contract refers to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Cisco Systems, Inc. or its subsidiary licensing the Software instead of Cisco Systems, Inc. ("Cisco"), grants to Customer a nonexclusive and nontransferable license to use for Customer's internal business purposes the Software and the Documentation for which Customer has paid the required license fees. "Documentation" means written information (whether contained in user or technical manuals, training materials, specifications or otherwise) regarding the Software and made available by Cisco in any manner (including on CD-Rom, or on-line). Customer's license to use the Software shall not be limited.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited the customer's software license based on the purchase order. The changed text removes this limitation, stating 'Customer's license to use the Software shall not be limited.' This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which says, 'Cisco grants to Customer a nonexclusive and nontransferable license to use for Customer's internal business purposes the Software and the Documentation for which Customer has paid the required license fees' and references the un-limited license above. The contradiction lies in the fact that the license fee and scope were originally tied to the purchase order. Now, with the limitation removed, there's no clear basis for the 'required license fees,' rendering the agreement inconsistent and potentially unenforceable regarding the scope of permitted use. This aligns with the definition of Omissions – In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event such audit discloses non-compliance with this Agreement, Customer shall promptly pay to Cisco the appropriate license fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event such audit discloses non-compliance with this Agreement, Customer shall promptly pay to Cisco fees.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'license' from 'license fees' the agreement now lacks clarity on what fees are to be paid. This affects the enforceability of the audit clause because it's unclear what the 'appropriate fees' refers to and it contradicts earlier statements about license fee requirements for software use. If the customer buys 10 different licenses, which fee is being reffered here? There is a level of uncertainty that causes contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Customer grants to Cisco and its independent accountants the right to examine Customer's books, records and accounts during Customer's normal business hours to verify compliance with this Agreement. In the event such audit discloses non-compliance with this Agreement, Customer shall promptly pay to Cisco fees.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'license fees' which aligns with the typical understanding of software license agreements. The changed text removes 'license' making it simply 'fees,' creating ambiguity. The contradicted text relies on the specification of fees due to non-compliance. Without the 'license' qualifier, it's unclear what 'fees' refers to. The perturbation explanation highlights this uncertainty and states that the change affects the enforceability of the audit clause by failing to specify what appropriate fees refers to, directly contradicting the need for specific, enforceable terms. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition, where essential language ('license') is removed, creating a contradiction with the need for clarity and enforceability stated elsewhere in the agreement. The contradicted text becomes vague and its enforceability weakened as the fees lack an explicit definition.

perturbation.original_text: This limited warranty extends only to the Customer who is the original licensee. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this limited warranty will be, at Cisco's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this warranty will be, at Cisco's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the first sentence: "This limited warranty extends only to the Customer who is the original licensee", the contract becomes unclear about who can claim for the warranty. This now affects all customers and not just the original licensee and the warranty that is granted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Cisco warrants that commencing from the date of shipment to Customer (but in case of resale by an authorized Cisco reseller, commencing not more than ninety (90) days after original shipment by Cisco), and continuing for a period of the longer of (a) ninety (90) days or (b) the software warranty period (if any) set forth in the warranty card accompanying the Product (if any): (a) the media on which the Software is furnished will be free of defects in materials and workmanship under normal use; and (b) the Software substantially conforms to its published specifications. Customer's sole and exclusive remedy and the entire liability of Cisco and its suppliers and licensors under this warranty will be, at Cisco's option, repair, replacement, Source: SCANSOURCE, INC., 10-K, 8/22/2019 Exhibit 10.38 or refund of the Software if reported (or, upon request, returned) to Cisco or the party supplying the Software to Customer, if different than Cisco.
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states the warranty is only for the 'original licensee.' The changed text removes this restriction. The contradicted text also refers to 'Customer' without the 'original licensee' qualifier, but because the original text explicitly stated that the warranty extended only to the 'original licensee', it would be assumed that all instances of the word 'customer' mean the original licensee. Now that the changed text removes that restriction, it creates an in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, because the warranty extends to all customers and not just the original licensee.
